notes.note: Commagene, the small client-kingdom on the upper Euphrates ruled by Antiochus I, was the natural corridor for a Parthian army crossing into the Roman province of Syria and Cilicia.

Magistrates leaving Rome to take up a military command put off the toga and donned the paludamentum, the general's cloak; the consuls dreaded a decree forcing them to do so for an unwanted Parthian war.

notes.anchor: any private citizen by decree of the Senate
notes.note: An extraordinary command voted to a privatus (a man holding no magistracy) --- as had been done for Pompey --- was the constitutionally dramatic option; Caelius notes that no one proposed it this time.

Translating velificari, 'to crowd on sail,' i.e. to trim one's report to catch a political wind; Caelius warns Cicero not to seem to be flattering Pompey or Caesar in what he writes home about the Parthians.
The ager Campanus, rich public land round Capua distributed under Caesar's law of 59 BC; control of its remaining allotments was a live political prize that Pompey now wished to deny Caesar.
Prorogatio was the extension of a governor's command beyond its term; Caelius cannot guarantee a successor for Cicero but promises at least to prevent his Cilician tenure being prolonged again.
